Water at her knees, one lantern between her and the encompassing darkness, Maren worked steadily upward toward the last of the choosing. Above her a grey suggestion of the upper landing revealed where the darkness thinned toward solid stone. She did not hurry, and she did not stop. Each step carried her a little further out of the drowned rooms, and a little nearer whatever the night still intended to ask of her. She made herself ascend deliberately, the way you carry a brimming cup, so that nothing she had gathered would spill. The archive was quieter now behind her, the low vaults surrendered to the sea, the argument between the town and the water settled in those rooms at least. What remained lay above, at the top of the stair, in the small dry space the flood had not yet managed to reach.

Whichever hand she had used, gentle or sudden, salvaging the little a single pair of hands could reach on a losing night came down now to the same thing: what she was willing to surrender. That was all it had ever been. Not skill, not strength, not the founder's method or her own, but the plain arithmetic of loss, this saved and that given up, and no way on earth to keep both. The choices behind her had narrowed to a single dripping stair and a single climb, and she felt the whole night funnel toward it. Maren stood a moment in the black water and let herself feel the weight of what she had already spent, and what she still carried, and the cold that had reached her bones. Then she found the bottom step with her boot. The way out was upward, through the flooded turns, toward the last of the choosing, and she took the first tread with the water pouring off her and the lantern guarding its small stubborn flame against the dark.

At the last landing of salvaging the little a single pair of hands could reach on a losing night, the water lapping the topmost dry step, Maren came to the weighing she had known was coming. It was the oldest question the night had asked, put now in its plainest form: whose claim came first, the town's or her own. She could put Saltmere first. She could set the whole of the town's memory above any single thing, herself included, and spend the last of the night in service of the many. Or she could put the one irreplaceable thing first, the single record that could never be remade, and let the broad claim of the town give way to the narrow, absolute claim of the thing that would otherwise be lost forever. Both were a kind of faithfulness. Neither was free. Maren stood on the streaming stone with the lantern low and the sea just beneath her feet and weighed the town entire against the one thing, the wide loss against the particular one, for the last time.

She put the town first. The moment she did, the salvage offered her a final bargain, in the voice the water employed, which was no voice at all but a pressure behind the eyes. She could pronounce the forgotten thing aloud. She could carry it up out of the archive and speak it to the whole of Saltmere in the grey morning, restoring the town's buried memory whether it desired the difficult gift or not, and let the speaking become the thing that finally broke the covenant open. That was the loud mercy, and also the dangerous one. Alternatively she could keep it and simply hold the line here. She could remain at the last dry step and refuse the water any further ground, preserving the memory without ever forcing it upon a soul, a quieter faithfulness that demanded less of the town and left more of it peacefully asleep. Maren weighed the spoken truth against the kept one, the public reckoning against the private vigil, for the last time.

Alone in the dark, she saved one page. She had trusted no one and asked for no help, and a single pair of hands in a drowning building can reach only so far; when the water finally drove her out she carried one sheet up the stair, and that was all the night had let her keep. The rest went under. She stood dripping on the high step with the one page in her fist and felt its smallness against everything the archive had held. The sea held its level a moment as though listening, and then took the last of the low rooms. Maren let the night close on the choice she had made, and could not pretend it was enough. She had been honest about a losing night and had lost, nearly all of it, and the one saved page in her hand was less a rescue than a receipt for everything the dark had taken while she worked alone.

ending.title: Alone in the Dark, One Page Saved

nodes.body: She went off the founder's map entirely, and kept a scrap. Verrin's method was no use on a night already lost, so she abandoned it and worked by instinct, and instinct, in the end, could save only a fragment, a single torn corner of a single record pulled from the water before it dissolved. It was almost nothing. It was also entirely her own, saved her own way, owing the founder not a thing. Below her the sea held its level a moment as though listening, then went on taking the rest. Maren let the night close on the choice she had made, the wet scrap pressed flat in her book, and found she could not weigh it. She had kept so little. But she had kept it without the dead man's hand guiding hers, and on a losing night, in a drowning archive, she was not certain that counted for nothing, and not certain it counted for much.

ending.title: Went Off the Founder’s Map and Kept a Scrap

nodes.body: She spoke to no one, and preserved only the corner. She could have called up the cliff for assistance, could have given the town its warning, and she did neither; she lowered her head and worked in silence and rescued a single corner of the archive while the remainder drowned unwitnessed. No one even knew what was lost. Below her the sea held its level a moment as though listening for a voice that never came, and then claimed everything she had not reached. Maren let the night close on the choice she had made, alone with her small salvage in the emptying darkness. She had preserved a corner and kept her silence, and the silence, she understood far too late, had cost more than the water did. A loss no one is told of is a loss suffered twice over. She had made it so, and there was no one remaining down here for her to say it to.

ending.title: Spoke to No One and Saved the Corner

nodes.body: She held the line until the line gave. She planted herself at the last dry step and swore the water would come no further, and for a long cold while it seemed she might be right; then the stone shifted beneath her, and the step went, and the sea came through all at once and took the room she had been guarding. She saved what she could seize in the moment the line broke, which was little, and hauled herself out with it. Below her the sea held its level a moment as though listening, and then filled the space where her line had been. Maren let the night close on the choice she had made, soaked and shaking on the higher stair. She had spent the night defending a thing she could not hold, and holding it had cost her the chance to save more, and the water had simply waited her out and won the ground the instant she faltered.

ending.title: Held the Line Till the Line Gave

nodes.body: She carried one page into the rain. When it became apparent the archive was lost, she selected a single record, took it in her hand, and went out the seaward door into the weather rather than permit it to disappear with the remainder. Up the cliff she carried it, the storm soaking it half to ruin, but it reached the high ground with her, one rescued page dripping in the grey and gathering dawn. It was not much at all. It was simply what she could carry alone. Below her the sea held its level a moment as though it were listening, then withdrew back down the rock, content with all it had already claimed. Maren let the night close on the choice she had made, standing on the headland with the rain easing and the one wet page cradled in her hands. She had salvaged what a single person can salvage, no more, and made her peace with the modest dimensions of it.

ending.title: Carried One Page Into the Rain

nodes.body: She sealed a vault that was already full of water. In her haste she wedged the door and banked the shelf and told herself she had saved the room, and only afterward, when the pressure bulged the seal and the dark seep began, did she understand the sea had reached it first. She had sealed the water in, not out. The record she meant to protect was already drowned behind the door she had shut so carefully. Below her the sea held its level a moment as though listening, almost, she thought, as though amused, and then went on. Maren let the night close on the choice she had made, standing before the sealed and useless vault with cold water running out under its door across her boots. She had done everything right and everything too late, and the neat shut door was the shape of her whole losing night: a keeping that kept nothing but the loss itself.

ending.title: Sealed a Vault That Was Already Full

nodes.body: She signed for a page, and the rest was sea. At the very last she set her name in the salvage log beside the one record she had managed to lift clear, made it official, made it hers; and when she looked up from the signing, the whole of the lower archive had gone under, quiet and complete, while she bent to write. Her signature sat there, precise and small, claiming a single sheet out of a drowned century. Below her the sea held its level a moment as though listening, and then took what little the dark still offered it. Maren let the night close on the choice she had made, the pen still in her cold hand, the log open to her own name and its one poor entry. She had wanted to answer honestly for the night, and the honest answer, signed and dated, was that she had saved one page and lost the sea's whole share of everything else.

ending.title: Signed for a Page and the Rest Was Sea

nodes.body: Unsigned, unsettled, but safe by dawn: that was how the night finally left her. She entered no name in the log and closed no account; she simply accomplished what a single pair of hands could accomplish, saved the little she could reach, and climbed out before the water took her with the rest. Nothing was resolved. The archive was mostly lost, the deeper questions still open, her own name still waiting on its impossible page in the darkness. But she was alive, and dry, and what she had preserved was genuinely real. Below her the sea held its level a moment as though it were listening, then eased down the cliff at last. Maren let the grey morning close on the choice she had made, standing spent on the high ground with her small honest salvage and no verdict to sign beneath it. Some nights conclude without an ending. This one merely ended, and she permitted it, and was glad to have survived it.

ending.title: Unsigned, Unsettled, but Safe by Dawn
